entities.description: The immediate printed source is Walter W. Seton's portion of a composite EETS volume. The 3rdFranRule component comprises an introduction (pp. 25-41), bibliography (p. 41), the English rule (pp. 45-55), the adjacent Latin Beatus Franciscus text from the same manuscript (pp. 55-57), notes (pp. 58-59), and a shared glossary (pp. 126-127).
entities.label: Seton 1914 edition of two fifteenth-century Franciscan rules
notes: Chambers edits only the opening courtesy-book work; Seton edits both Franciscan rules and supplies their apparatus.

rights.basis: The edition was first published in 1914, more than the 95-year maximum term described by the U.S. Copyright Office for pre-1978 statutory copyrights; U-M also currently labels the item public domain in the United States.
rights.component: The medieval English and Latin texts, Seton's 1914 transcription and apparatus, and the two published manuscript plates

entities.description: A nineteen-leaf illustrated vellum devotional manuscript containing the English rule at fols. 2r-15v, an added Latin extract from Bernardine de Bustis at fols. 15v-17, and prayers and miniatures at fols. 17v-19. It was formerly in Thomas Pennant's Downing collection, was purchased by Seton after the March 1913 Sotheby's sale, and is now held by the National Library of Wales.

holding.notes: The exact NLW component appears in the official Archives and Manuscripts result page under 'Rule of the Third Order of St Francis'.

entities.label: National Library of Wales MS 22873B

physicalDescription.extent: 19 leaves; the English rule occupies fols. 2r-15v
notes: The English rule is in a good bastard secretary script; Seton describes it as a neat, legible English hand with generally nineteen lines per page.
Three full-page miniatures survive at fols. 1v, 17v, and 18v; leaves for five intervening Offices were apparently cut out for their miniatures.
The current NLW catalog calls the Latin addition contemporary and in similar script; Seton calls it a later addition in a different, smaller, apparently Italian hand.
Seton's introductory sentence says the English version occupies leaves 2-14, but his edition and the current catalog show that the ending continues through fol. 15v.

reviewNotes: The cataloged work is the Middle English rule on NLW MS 22873B fols. 2r-15v, not the whole manuscript, Nicholas IV's Latin source, or the complete 1914 book.
The current NLW catalog resolves the XML's historical 'whereabouts unknown' note: the former Pennant and Seton witness is extant as NLW MS 22873B.
Seton's volume is composite: R. W. Chambers edits the courtesy book, while Walter W. Seton edits the two Franciscan rules. Only Seton has responsibility for 3rdFranRule.
The U-M encoding was generated by scanning and OCR; no item-specific statement establishing full manual correction was found.
U-M's current No Copyright-United States statement and the restrictive 1999 XML availability notice are preserved separately rather than reconciled by assumption.

lineage.summary: The repository artifact is a local CME-submodule representation of University of Michigan item 3rdFranRule, produced in 1999 by scanning and ScanWorx OCR from Walter W. Seton's contribution to EETS Original Series 148 (1914). Seton transcribed the English rule from the former Pennant and Seton manuscript, now National Library of Wales MS 22873B, fols. 2r-15v. That Middle English version translates the rule incorporated in Pope Nicholas IV's 1289 bull Supra montem. The U-M selection retains the edition's component title page, chapter table, and twenty-four-chapter English text but omits Seton's introduction, endnotes, glossary, and the manuscript's following Latin extract. A complete scan of the 1914 composite book is public through Internet Archive; within it Seton reproduces only manuscript fols. 9v and 15v, not a complete manuscript facsimile.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous Middle English translator; Latin source promulgated by Nicholas IV
catalogSummary.date: Translation undated; attested in a manuscript of around 1500
catalogSummary.region: England; exact place of translation and copying unknown
catalogSummary.title: The Thirde Order of Seynt Franceys for the Brethren and Susters of the Order of Penitentis

catalogingSubject.label: The Thirde Order of Seynt Franceys for the Brethren and Susters of the Order of Penitentis
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The anonymous Middle English translation and version of the rule incorporated in Pope Nicholas IV's 1289 bull Supra montem, attested in National Library of Wales MS 22873B at fols. 2r-15v. The record does not describe the Latin source rule, the whole nineteen-leaf manuscript, its adjacent Latin extract and Offices, Seton's composite 1914 EETS volume, the University of Michigan encoding, or the repository XML artifact.

contentParts.label: Table of the twenty-four chapters
notes: The table repeats the twenty-four chapters and therefore contributes no additional unique numbered item to the work-level extent.
contentParts.summary: A rubric and twenty-four entries preview the rule's chapter sequence.

contentParts.label: Chapter I: Catholic faith
contentParts.summary: The papal preamble affirms Roman Catholic faith and invokes Francis as founder of the order.

contentParts.label: Chapter II: Entry into the rule
contentParts.summary: Candidates are examined for faith, obedience, status, restitution, and reconciliation before admission.

contentParts.label: Chapter III: Profession
contentParts.summary: After a year, a suitable candidate makes a formally recorded promise and profession.

contentParts.label: Chapter IV: Permanence in the rule
contentParts.summary: Professed members may not return to the world, while married women require their husbands' consent.

contentParts.label: Chapter V: Clothing
contentParts.summary: The rule prescribes modest clothing and limits costly fabrics, fur, silk, and worldly display.

contentParts.label: Chapter VI: Entertainments
contentParts.summary: Members avoid dishonest feasts, spectacles, courts, dances, and support for minstrels' vanities.

contentParts.label: Chapter VII: Abstinence from meat
contentParts.summary: The rule defines weekly meat abstinence, exceptions, ordinary meals, temperance, and meal prayers.

contentParts.label: Chapter VIII: Fasting
contentParts.summary: Members observe weekly and seasonal fasts with exceptions for illness, pregnancy, travel, and labor.

contentParts.label: Chapter IX: Confession and communion
contentParts.summary: Members confess, receive communion, reconcile with neighbors, and restore others' goods at three annual feasts.

contentParts.label: Chapter X: Weapons
contentParts.summary: Brothers may not bear weapons except for specified defensive purposes or by ministerial license.

contentParts.label: Chapter XI: Prayer
contentParts.summary: Clerical and unlettered members receive daily offices, psalms, creeds, and Pater Noster obligations with allowances for sickness.

contentParts.label: Chapter XII: Testaments
contentParts.summary: Legally capable members must make a testament within three months of entry.

contentParts.label: Chapter XIII: Keeping peace
contentParts.summary: Ministers pursue peace within and beyond the fraternity and seek episcopal counsel in legal conflicts.

contentParts.label: Chapter XIV: Oaths
contentParts.summary: Members avoid solemn and casual oaths except in defined lawful circumstances and perform penance for careless swearing.

contentParts.label: Chapter XV: Hearing Mass
contentParts.summary: Healthy members hear Mass daily when possible and attend a designated monthly solemn Mass.

contentParts.label: Chapter XVI: Almsgiving
contentParts.summary: Regular contributions support poor, sick, and unburied members, other poor people, the church, and religious instruction.

contentParts.label: Chapter XVII: Silence in church
contentParts.summary: Members keep silence and attend to prayer and preaching during Mass, subject to fraternity needs.

contentParts.label: Chapter XVIII: Sick and dead members
contentParts.summary: Ministers visit and support the sick; members attend funerals and perform specified prayers and Masses for the dead.

contentParts.label: Chapter XIX: Holding office
contentParts.summary: Members faithfully undertake assigned offices, which are limited in duration rather than held for life.

contentParts.label: Chapter XX: Visitation
contentParts.summary: The fraternity gathers for priestly visitation, preferably by Friars Minor, with annual correction and expulsion after repeated warning.

contentParts.label: Chapter XXI: Strife and disputes
contentParts.summary: Brothers and sisters avoid disputes, make amends, or answer before lawful jurisdiction.

contentParts.label: Chapter XXII: Dispensation from fasting
contentParts.summary: Ordinaries and visitors may dispense members from fasting and observances for reasonable need.

contentParts.label: Chapter XXIII: Incorrigible members
contentParts.summary: Ministers report open faults; the visitor punishes and may expel an incorrigible member after three warnings.

contentParts.label: Chapter XXIV: Moral force and conclusion
contentParts.summary: The rule distinguishes its observances from mortal-sin obligations, prescribes penance for offenses, and closes with the bull's prohibition, sanction, place, and date.

dateStatements.display: No later than about 1500; otherwise undated
notes: The witness date supplies only a terminus ante quem for the translation represented by that copy; it is not treated as an exact translation date.
This dates the extant manuscript witness, not composition of the 1289 Latin source and not a securely identified translation event.

extent.description: The encoded work has a twenty-four-entry table followed by twenty-four chapter divisions containing 28 body paragraphs. It preserves 28 manuscript leaf-side milestones (2r-15v) and 11 print-page milestones (45-55). These are representation metrics for the selected U-M XML, not a count of the whole manuscript or whole 1914 volume.

formStatements.description: A prose rule organized as a table of chapters, papal preamble, and twenty-four prose chapters; short embedded Latin formulas do not change the whole-work form.

genres.label: Papal bull
notes: The cataloged English version preserves the address and closing of Nicholas IV's bull and translates the rule incorporated in it.

languageStatements.label: Latin
notes: The separate Latin Beatus Franciscus extract on witness fols. 15v-17 is outside the cataloged work and omitted by the encoding.

scope.description: Prayer names, psalm incipits, liturgical phrases, and the closing Deo gracias embedded in the English rule

notes: The XML TITLE TYPE=130 value 'Fifteenth-century courtesy book. Selections' belongs to cataloging of the composite 1914 volume and is not treated as the medieval work's title.
The XML language profile declares Latin but omits the overwhelmingly primary Middle English; the manifest corrects scope from direct text inspection rather than copying that incomplete header field.
R. W. Chambers edited the courtesy-book component of EETS OS 148, not the cataloged Franciscan rule; Walter W. Seton edited the two Franciscan components.

Copyright, provider terms, reproduction access, and the conflict between U-M's current No Copyright-United States label and the embedded 1999 availability notice are recorded in the linked lineage manifest.

notes: Seton treats the version as made for English Tertiaries but reports no clue to its precise place of production. Rieti is the issue place of the Latin source bull, London the edition's publication place, and Aberystwyth the current holding place; none fixes the translation place.
Source-work responsibility only; this does not attribute the English wording to Nicholas IV or erase the rule's earlier textual history.

subjects.label: Religious life of brothers and sisters of penance
Fasting, prayer, confession, communion, and almsgiving
Admission, profession, visitation, discipline, and community governance

summaries.value: An anonymous Middle English version of the rule promulgated by Pope Nicholas IV in Supra montem (1289) for brothers and sisters of the Order of Penance. Its twenty-four chapters regulate Catholic profession, admission, clothing, diet and fasting, worship and prayer, property and peace, oaths and weapons, almsgiving, care of sick and dead members, offices, visitation, discipline, and the rule's moral force.

NLW MS 22873B is a composite devotional manuscript: the English rule at fols. 2r-15v is followed by a Latin extract from Bernardine de Bustis at fols. 15v-17 and by prayer Offices and miniatures at fols. 17v-19, with leaves for five Offices now missing. Seton's 1914 component prints the English rule and Latin extract, but the U-M encoding selects the English rule and omits Seton's introduction, endnotes, the Latin extract, and glossary.

Its twenty-four chapters regulate Catholic profession, admission, clothing, diet and fasting, worship and prayer, property and peace, oaths and weapons, almsgiving, care of sick and dead members, offices, visitation, discipline, and the rule's moral force.
